source.title: Engineering a vitamin B12 high-throughput screening system by riboswitch sensor in Sinorhizobium meliloti

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Low VB12 yield despite gene overexpression; need for high-throughput screening method to identify hyperproducing mutants from random mutagenesis libraries.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: VB12 biosynthesis requires ~30 genes; overexpression did not increase yield. High-yield strains obtained by mutagenesis need efficient screening. A riboswitch library was screened, btuB from S. typhimurium identified as best. Flow cytometry HTS system developed. ARTP mutagenesis optimized. Mutant MC5-2 obtained with 21.9% higher titer (156 mg/L vs 128 mg/L). Genome sequenced, mutations analyzed. First riboswitch use in S. meliloti.
process_signals: Problem: overexpression of ~30 genes did not increase VB12 yield
Need: efficient screening approach for mutants
Approach: riboswitch library construction and screening
Key result: btuB riboswitch identified as best regulatory device
Outcome: HTS system developed, mutant MC5-2 with 21.9% higher titer

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: VB12 is complex, requires >30 enzymes. Traditional screening methods have low dynamic range and throughput. FACS enables ultrahigh throughput but needs a biosensor. Riboswitches are natural metabolite sensors that regulate gene expression upon ligand binding. ~200 VB12 riboswitch elements identified across bacteria. Goal: screen a sensitive riboswitch to sense intracellular VB12 by FACS and develop HTS system for S. meliloti mutants.
process_signals: Constraint: targeted engineering (overexpression/deletion) often fails due to metabolic complexity
Hypothesis: riboswitches can be engineered as VB12 sensors to couple intracellular VB12 to fluorescent reporter
Design: screen riboswitch candidates from ~200 known elements
Goal: develop FACS-based HTS for VB12 hyperproducers
failures_or_limits: Traditional screening methods suffer from low dynamic range, low throughput, sensitivity to variable parameters

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Methods section placeholder; no detailed text provided.

section_gists.heading: Analytical methods
section_gists.gist: Biomass measured at OD600. VB12 quantified by HPLC with C18 column, water/acetonitrile mobile phase, detection at 361 nm. Sample preparation includes NaNO2, acetic acid, boiling, filtration, NaCN addition. Strains and plasmids listed: Sm320 wild type, Sm320-L6 (hemE overexpression, low producer), Sm320-N8 (cobI deletion, non-producer), various riboswitch plasmids with gfp reporter, dual-repression plasmids (SYB-lacI, pMB-gfp).
process_signals: Engineered strains with different VB12 production capacities for sensor testing
HPLC method for accurate VB12 quantification
Plasmid constructs: riboswitch-gfp fusions, dual-repression system
methods: Plasmid construction: riboswitch-gfp fusions, lacI repressor system

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Results section placeholder; no detailed text provided.

section_gists.heading: Engineered mutants with different production capacities of VB12
section_gists.gist: To evaluate riboswitch dose-response, mutants with varying VB12 production were created: Sm320-N8 (cobI deletion, no VB12), Sm320-L6 (hemE overexpression, low VB12). HPLC confirmed VB12 levels: Sm320 126.28 mg/L total, 18.5 mg/L intracellular; Sm320-L6 65.21 mg/L total, 9.65 mg/L intracellular; Sm320-N8 0 mg/L. Intracellular VB12 used as indicator for sensor testing.
process_signals: Design: create strains with different intracellular VB12 levels to test riboswitch response
Observation: intracellular VB12 correlates with total production
Note: intracellular VB12 measured, not real concentration
methods: Gene deletion (cobI) to abolish VB12 production
Overexpression of hemE to reduce VB12 production
HPLC quantification of total and intracellular VB12
failures_or_limits: Intracellular VB12 measurement may not reflect real concentration

section_gists.heading: Construction of riboswitch-based VB12 sensors
section_gists.gist: Eight riboswitch candidates selected from different bacteria (Table 2). Plasmids with riboswitch-gfp fusions transformed into Sm320, Sm320-L6, Sm320-N8. Flow cytometry after 3-day fermentation. Five riboswitches showed similar fluorescence regardless of VB12 level (non-responsive). Three showed correlation: SY-btuB, EC-btuB, and one other showed negative correlation (repression). SY-btuB selected as most sensitive for further development.
process_signals: Hypothesis: riboswitches will respond to intracellular VB12 levels
Experiment: test 8 riboswitch-gfp constructs in strains with different VB12 levels
Partial failure: 5 riboswitches showed no differential response
Success: SY-btuB showed clear dose-response (negative correlation, i.e., repression)
Decision: further examine SY-btuB as sensor
methods: Cloning riboswitch-gfp fusions under constitutive promoter
failures_or_limits: 5 out of 8 riboswitches did not respond to VB12 levels
Negative correlation (repression) not ideal for screening (want high fluorescence for high producers)

section_gists.heading: Examination of the SY-btuB riboswitch sensor
section_gists.gist: SY-btuB is a repressive riboswitch (high VB12 → low GFP). To invert signal, dual-repression mode: SY-btuB controls lacI repressor, which represses gfp. Thus, high VB12 → low LacI → high GFP. Tested in Sm320, Sm320-L6, Sm320-N8: positive correlation between GFP and VB12 productivity. Flow cytometry showed clear separation. Sensor validated for screening.
process_signals: Problem: repressive riboswitch gives inverse correlation
Revision: dual-repression mode with lacI to invert signal
Validation: positive correlation achieved, tested in three strains
Outcome: sensor ready for HTS
methods: Construction of SY-btuB-lacI fusion and lacO-gfp reporter
Testing in strains with different VB12 levels
failures_or_limits: Initial single-reporter sensor had inverse correlation, requiring redesign

section_gists.heading: High throughput screening of mutants using riboswitch
section_gists.gist: ARTP mutagenesis optimized: 60 s exposure, 80-85% lethality. Mutant library (~6x10^7 mutants) screened with dual-repression SY-btuB sensor. Top 0.1% fluorescence selected. After 3 rounds, 144 colonies picked; 58.3% had higher VB12 than wild type in 24-well plates. Six mutants >10% increase. In flasks, MC5-2 produced 156±4.2 mg/L (21.82% higher than wild type). Stable after 7 generations.
process_signals: ARTP condition optimization (lethality curve)
Application of sensor to screen random mutant library
Enrichment: 58.3% of selected mutants had higher VB12
Best mutant MC5-2: 21.82% increase, stable

section_gists.heading: Whole genome sequencing analyses
section_gists.gist: Genome of MC5-2 sequenced: 17 SNPs, 1 Indel. Mutations in VB12 pathway, heme branch, ABC transporters, precursor synthesis, TCA cycle. SNPs may alter transcription or enzyme activity, enhancing VB12 flux or weakening competing branches. Provides insights for metabolic engineering.
process_signals: Goal: identify genetic basis of hyperproduction
Finding: multiple mutations in relevant pathways
Implication: unexpected mutations can guide rational engineering
failures_or_limits: Causal link between specific mutations and phenotype not experimentally validated

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: First application of riboswitch sensor for metabolite screening. Culture conditions affect titer; medium optimized for FACS screening (replaced CSL with AYP/peptone to avoid interference). Sensor validated by screening higher producers. Limitations: sensor may not be universal; only tested in S. meliloti; mutations may have off-target effects. Future: use identified mutations for metabolic engineering.
process_signals: Constraint: medium composition affects sensor performance (CSL sediment interferes with FACS)
Revision: optimized medium for screening
Limitation: sensor specificity and host range not tested
Future direction: rational engineering based on identified mutations
failures_or_limits: Sensor may not work in other organisms
Off-target effects of mutations not fully characterized
Medium optimization required for FACS compatibility

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: Developed VB12 HTS system using riboswitch sensor. ARTP mutant library screened; MC5-2 showed 21.82% higher yield. Genome sequencing revealed mutations. New paradigm for HTS and elucidating unexpected mutations for metabolic engineering.
process_signals: Achievement: first riboswitch-based HTS for VB12
Impact: provides method for strain improvement and understanding of VB12 regulation

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Initial problem: VB12 yield not improved by overexpression of biosynthetic genes, likely due to complex regulation.
Hypothesis: riboswitches can be repurposed as intracellular VB12 sensors to enable FACS-based screening.
Design: test multiple riboswitch candidates; use dual-repression to invert signal for positive selection.
Validation: engineered strains with different VB12 levels used to calibrate sensor response.
Application: ARTP mutagenesis + sensor screening yields hyperproducer; genome sequencing reveals target genes for future engineering.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why did 5 out of 8 riboswitches fail to respond? Were they non-functional in S. meliloti or did they require different conditions?
What is the dynamic range and detection limit of the SY-btuB sensor?
How specific is the sensor for VB12 vs. other corrinoids?
Can the sensor be transferred to other VB12-producing species?
What are the exact mechanisms by which the identified SNPs increase VB12 production?
Were there any false positives or negatives in the screening?
How does the dual-repression system affect cell fitness or VB12 production?

events.summary: A riboswitch library was constructed from ~200 known VB12 elements and screened in engineered strains with varied VB12 production.
Eight riboswitch candidates were tested in strains with different VB12 levels; five showed no response, three showed correlation.
The btuB riboswitch from Salmonella typhimurium (SY-btuB) was identified as the best regulatory device.
A flow cytometry high-throughput screening system was developed based on the btuB riboswitch.
ARTP mutagenesis was optimized by determining lethality rate at different treatment times.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers abstract, background, and methods sections, providing key problem statement, hypothesis, design of riboswitch library, initial screening, and ARTP optimization.

events.summary: Tested eight riboswitch-GFP constructs in strains with different VB12 levels by flow cytometry.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers design of test strains, riboswitch screening, failure of most candidates, and successful redesign of SY-btuB sensor.

events.summary: The dual-repression sensor was validated by mixing three strains with different VB12 levels; FACS sorted top 1% fluorescence, and 84% were the high-producing Sm320 strain.
ARTP mutagenesis was optimized with 60 s exposure achieving 80-85% lethality, and a mutant library of ~6x10^7 mutants was screened using the riboswitch sensor.
Whole genome sequencing of MC5-2 identified 17 SNPs and 1 Indel in VB12-related pathways, suggesting mutations that enhance VB12 flux or weaken competing branches.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains key experimental results: riboswitch screening, dual-repression design, sensor validation, ARTP mutagenesis, mutant screening, and genome analysis.

events.summary: Whole genome sequencing of MC5-2 identified gene mutations potentially contributing to VB12 hyperproduction.
Mutations in heme branch pathway (hemin, hemN) may reduce heme synthesis, increasing VB12 productivity.
Developed HTS system successfully selected hyperproducing mutant, providing new paradigm for screening.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains results on mutant characterization, genome sequencing, and conclusions.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Low vitamin B12 yield in Sinorhizobium meliloti despite gene overexpression; need for a high-throughput screening method to identify hyperproducing mutants from random mutagenesis libraries.

canonical_events.summary: Eight riboswitch-GFP constructs tested in the three engineered strains; five showed no differential response, three (including SY-btuB) showed VB12-dependent repression.
SY-btuB from Salmonella typhimurium identified as the most sensitive VB12-responsive riboswitch, but it acts as a repressor (high VB12 → low GFP).
Dual-repression sensor validated by mixing three strains and FACS sorting top 1% fluorescence; 84% of sorted cells were the high-producing Sm320 strain.
ARTP mutagenesis conditions optimized: 60 s exposure gave 80-85% lethality, used to generate a mutant library of ~6×10^7 mutants.
In 24-well plate fermentation, 58.3% (84/144) of selected mutants produced higher VB12 than wild-type; six mutants showed >10% increase.
In flask fermentation, mutant MC5-2 produced 156±4.2 mg/L VB12, a 21.82% increase over wild-type (128 mg/L), and remained stable after 7 generations (154±3.2 mg/L).
Whole genome sequencing of MC5-2 identified 17 SNPs and 1 Indel in VB12 pathway, heme branch, ABC transporters, precursor synthesis, and TCA cycle, suggesting mechanisms for hyperproduction.
Sensor specificity and host range not tested; causal link between specific mutations and phenotype not experimentally validated; medium optimization required for FACS compatibility.
First application of riboswitch sensor for metabolite screening in S. meliloti, providing a new paradigm for HTS and guiding metabolic engineering.

edges.reason: Screening identifies SY-btuB as best candidate.
Validated sensor enables mutagenesis and screening workflow.
Top mutants validated in flask fermentation, MC5-2 identified as best.
Hyperproducer MC5-2 sequenced to identify causative mutations.
Sequencing reveals mutations but limitations remain in causal validation and sensor generality.
Despite limitations, the study validates the overall approach as a new paradigm.

must_cover_points: Problem of low VB12 yield despite overexpression
Hypothesis of using riboswitches as sensors
Construction of engineered strains with varied VB12 levels
Screening of riboswitch candidates and identification of SY-btuB
Design of dual-repression system to invert signal
Validation of sensor by sorting mixed strains
Identification of MC5-2 with 21.82% higher yield
